clean_story:
Underground warning networks. Trees alert neighbors of death.

The phrase "wood wide web" names the fungal threads linking tree roots—a silent internet of soil. When a tree is injured or dying, it pumps distress signals through these threads: chemical warnings like a burst of bitter sap or electric pulses, slower than nerve impulses but just as urgent.

Here’s how it works: A pine under beetle attack floods its leaves with toxic resins and sends sugar-starved alerts via fungi to nearby birches. The birches, sensing danger, preemptively boost their own defenses—tannins that make their bark unappetizing. Even stumps, long assumed dead, stay weakly connected; researchers found carbon flowing from living pines into leafless stumps for decades, like a fading heartbeat on life support.

Dying trees don’t just warn—they feed. As roots decay, they leak nutrients into the network, a final gift to seedlings. One study tracked radioactive carbon from a dying Douglas fir moving into neighboring cedars within days. The forest treats death as a shared event, not an endpoint.

Three related subjects are fungal intelligence, plant electrical signals, and how ecosystems recycle crisis.
